quotes.verbatim_quote: Diads, also known as double-stops, are two-note harmonizations. They work very well for chord tones and non-chord tones. Any interval is possible, but 3rds and 6ths are the most common.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the foundational technique of two-note harmonization and specifies the preferred intervals for practical application.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Very often we add a non-chord tone that is in the melody to the chord. This will change the name of the chord, as you will be adding higher extensions and altered tones. This is a common way to come up with enhancements.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Teaches the core technique of chord extension through melody integration and explains how this creates chord alterations.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Voice leading is the term we use to describe the movement of individual voices from one
chord to another. This is very important when choosing voicings for a chord melody
arrangement.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the essential concept of voice leading and establishes its critical role in chord-melody arranging.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Good voice-leading often means
arranging your chords so that the voices actually travel in whole and half steps.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Provides the key principle for creating smooth, idiomatic voice leading through stepwise motion.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Usually, it
is the 3rd and the 7th degrees of the chords. This is because chords are defined by their
3rds and 7ths which differentiate major, minor and dominant chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains why the 3rd and 7th are the most important chord tones for voice leading, as they define chord quality.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A passing chord is a chord added between two others to create a smoother transition.
Very often you can harmonize both chord and non-chord tones as passing chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the technique of using intermediate harmonies to smooth chord progressions.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
One common application of passing chords is the use of a dominant chord (altered or
unaltered) with a root that is a half step higher, or lower, than the chord being approached.
These chords can end up being non-diatonic, or from outside the key.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains a primary technique for applying dominant passing chords and permits non-diatonic harmony for smoothness.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
diminished chords repeat themselves every three frets, so you can choose from
among many possible locations on the fingerboard.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Provides a practical technical insight for locating diminished chord voicings on the guitar.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Approaching the | chord with a diminished chord from one half step below the root is a
common substitute for the V7. The reason this works is that the two chords have three
notes in common and both resolve nicely to I.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the harmonic equivalence between diminished and dominant seventh chords, enabling practical substitution.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In conventional harmony, we use chords that are constructed primarily from stacking 3rds.
In quartal harmony, chords are constructed by stacking 4ths.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the alternative harmony system based on fourths rather than thirds.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Some players prefer a chord/melody style with fewer chords. They will play mostly single
notes and add chords only when the chord changes. Sometimes this is the best way to deal
with chord tones and non-chord tones alike.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Presents an alternative minimalist approach to harmonizing non-chord tones.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Doubling melody notes in octaves will create a stronger effect than single notes, and some-
times you might find this kind of emphasis desirable. Listen to Wes Montgomery and
George Benson to hear great examples of this kind of playing.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Teaches the technique of octave doubling and provides authoritative models for the style.
